versions.name: RXV2-0.9.77-mdns-reannounce
versions.notes: Fixes <hostname>.local going dark after a reconnect/reboot, which looked like 'lost WiFi password / dropped to AP' but wasn't — the credentials were always safe in NVS and STA had actually rejoined the home network; only mDNS had failed to re-announce. mDNS was first started in startWifiStation() before the STA interface had an IP, so it bound to the soft-AP side and the name wouldn't resolve on the home network. Now onWifiConnected() tears mDNS down and re-announces it (http service on :80) on every (re)connect, once a real STA address exists — so PT2.local / PT1A.local etc. come back promptly after any reboot or reconnect. Includes everything from 0.9.76 (the model-ID broadcast-until-flown fix). Note: the home-page 'firmware up to date' banner fix still requires a LittleFS filesystem upload (uploadfs), not just an OTA firmware flash.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.76-id-confirm
versions.notes: Model-ID stability — final. The receiver now broadcasts its board ID on the shared ack slots whenever the model isn't being flown, with NO time cap (the Model IDs / identify screen holds a connection up indefinitely, so any finite window could be outwaited — that was the remaining failure). 'Being flown' is detected from genuine stick movement: a control channel must leave a 200-count deadband from its first-seen value on this connection, confirmed across 3 packets so a single 2.4 GHz-corrupted packet (WiFi sharing the band with the nRF24 on the bench) can't trip it. Take-off stick movement still hands over to telemetry + FHSS within ~1 ack. Result: rock-steady ID on the identify screen (verified 14/14 consecutive reads on the three-transceiver board), correct telemetry in flight. In normal flight WiFi is off (Fly mode) so the link is clean regardless. Also exposes id_broadcasting / being_flown in /api/state.json. Reminder: the home-page 'firmware up to date' banner fix (0.9.70) only lands when the LittleFS filesystem is re-uploaded, not via an OTA firmware flash.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.73-id-grace
versions.notes: Hardens the 0.9.72 'broadcast the model ID until the model is being flown' detector, which was tripping falsely. The transmitter only sends CHANGED channels, so the 16 channel values fill in over the first few packets of a connection (0 → mid-scale); 0.9.72 snapped its stick baseline at the first packet and a 50-count deadband, so that initial fill-in looked like a giant stick movement and immediately flagged 'being flown' — dropping the receiver back to telemetry on the shared ack slots, which the unmatched 'Model IDs' screen then displayed as an ever-changing ID. Fix: a 2 s settling grace during which the baseline keeps refreshing (so it captures fully-populated, at-rest stick positions) and 'being flown' cannot latch, plus a wider 200-count deadband (well above gimbal jitter, well below a deliberate stick move). Net: idle on the Model IDs screen → the ID stays rock-steady; on take-off a real stick move ends the broadcast within ~one ack; 60 s backstop cap; re-armed every reconnection. Also surfaces id_broadcasting and being_flown in /api/state.json for diagnostics. NOTE: the home-page 'firmware up to date' banner fix (0.9.70) is a web-asset change that only lands when the receiver's LittleFS filesystem is re-uploaded, not via an OTA firmware flash — that filesystem upload is being done alongside this release.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.72-id-until-flown
versions.notes: Completes the model-ID stability story for the 'Model IDs' (identify/store) screen. 0.9.71 made the MAC broadcast dense and swap-proof, but only for a fixed 3 s after each connection — and the 'Model IDs' screen keeps the transmitter permanently UN-matched (it reads the incoming ID continuously so you can store it), so once the 3 s lapsed the TX fell through to reading our normal telemetry on the two shared ack slots: slot 0 = firmware version + active-transceiver index, slot 1 = received-packet count. Both change as the three radios cycle, which is exactly the 'different ID every time' that was seen. The shared slots are unavoidable — a MATCHED (flying) transmitter must read them as version/packets, an UNMATCHED one reads them as the model ID, and the receiver can't see which state the TX is in. Fix: broadcast the ID densely until there is positive evidence the model is being FLOWN — namely a control channel moving past a 50-count deadband from its value when the link came up — then switch to telemetry. Idle on the identify screen (sticks still) → the ID stays rock-steady; on take-off the throttle/cyclic moves and telemetry resumes within about one ack, exactly like v1 (which delivered the MAC only for the first 20 acks of each connection). Re-armed on every reconnection, with a 60 s backstop cap. Verified against the v1 transmitter's ParseAckPayload/GetModelsMacAddress and CompareVersionNumbers (which safely ignores non-version bytes, so a flying TX is never mis-warned).

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.71-mac-window-3rx
versions.notes: Finishes the MAC-stability fix for three-transceiver receivers. 0.9.70 re-armed the MAC broadcast on every reconnection, which cured single-transceiver boards — but a three-transceiver board kept reporting a different ID each connection. Root cause: swapRadios() fires loadNextAck() three extra times per swap, so the old ack-COUNT-based MAC burst (first 20 acks) was exhausted almost instantly, and the post-burst MAC was sparse — slots 0/1 carried a MAC half only twice per ~37-item telemetry cycle. With three radios cycling, the transmitter often failed to collect BOTH 4-byte halves on whichever transceiver it was hearing before the cycle moved on, so it never assembled a full 8-byte ID, never model-matched, and then misread the cycling telemetry (slot 0 = active-transceiver index, slot 1 = packet count — both change as the radios swap) as an ever-changing MAC. Single-radio boards never swap, so they were fine. Fix: the MAC phase is now a wall-clock WINDOW (3 s from each fresh connection) that can't be exhausted by swap-injected calls, and it sends a MAC half on EVERY ack (dense alternation) rather than 2-in-37 — so both halves land within milliseconds regardless of how the radios swap. After the window, telemetry resumes exactly as before. v1 only ever had two transceivers, so this failure mode is new to V2's triple-transceiver design.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.70-mac-burst-fwcheck
versions.notes: Two long-standing bugs squashed for good, both root-caused as regressions from earlier fixes. (1) Firmware-update check wrongly said 'up to date' when a newer build was available: the home-page banner's version comparison used the opposite sign convention from the /firmware page and compared installed-vs-latest in the wrong order, so a genuine update fell into the 'up to date' branch. An earlier fix had corrected an inverted *sort*; this was an inverted *final compare* left over from the same refactor. (2) Receiver reported an unstable MAC to V1 transmitters: the chip's stored board MAC was always fine, but the V1 TX reads ack-payload slots 0/1 as the MAC until it model-matches, and the 20-ack MAC broadcast burst was gated by a counter that was only ever incremented, never reset — so the burst ran once at first boot and never again. After that the receiver permanently put firmware-version on slot 0 and the live packet count on slot 1, which an unmatched TX (a model not yet saved on it) latched as a garbage, ever-changing address. V1 receivers reset that counter on every link loss so each reconnection re-delivered a clean MAC; the V2 rewrite dropped the reset. Restored: the MAC burst now re-arms on every fresh connection (a >500 ms ack gap, the same threshold the output stage already treats as failsafe).

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.69-crsf-cold-boot
versions.notes: Regression fix for 0.9.68. On a cold CRSF boot the failsafe-detach logic (added in 0.9.68 so the LED could warn on link loss) tripped immediately because lastChannelDataMs was still zero, tearing down Serial1 before the FC had a chance to send its first telemetry frame. Serial1 is bidirectional on CRSF — when D6 TX went, D5 RX went with it, and the chip never saw any CRSF telemetry, which hid the Rotorflight config button on the home page. The fix is a one-liner: only allow the detach AFTER the link has been live at least once, so 'no link' is genuinely 'we had a link and lost it' rather than 'we just powered up'.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.68-bind-banner
versions.notes: Two improvements that go together. (1) Unmissable BIND MODE banner on the home page whenever the receiver is unbound — pulses amber, explains what the receiver is waiting for, and pairs with the 2 Hz LED blink so you have a clear cue both on-screen and on the hardware. (2) LED status now works across protocols, not just SBUS: on idle-HIGH protocols like CRSF and IBUS the firmware now releases the UART during failsafe (matches CRSF convention — FC infers loss from absence of frames) so the on-board LED can be driven LOW as a 'no link' warning. The LED reads: BLINKING = bind mode, LIT = something needs attention (bind or restore link), DARK = link is fine on idle-HIGH protocols. SBUS/FBUS users still see solid-on when bound. Also: the bind-page 'Rebooting' panel now explains explicitly that bind mode is about to be active and what the LED will look like.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.67-status-led
versions.notes: On-board LED now encodes link state at a glance: OFF = bound but no RC packet received in the last 250 ms (link down or TX off), SOLID ON = bound and receiving from the TX right now, BLINKING at 2 Hz = unbound, listening for a bind packet. Replaces the 50 ms heartbeat-every-2 s pattern. Caveat: on the XIAO ESP32-S3 the on-board LED shares GPIO 21 with SBUS-TX, so during normal flight the LED appears slightly dim/flickery as the UART peripheral drives the pin between our digitalWrite calls — that's expected and actually doubles as 'frames going out' confirmation. Add a window in the case or a light pipe to see it from outside.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.66-silent-unbound
versions.notes: Fixes a hard TX lockup with V1 transmitters when a V2 receiver is unbound and present. Diagnosed as a latent V1 TX firmware bug: when the TX scans the default pipe and receives V2's first few autoACKs (which carry our 8-byte board MAC, dynamic length) its parser locks up and the transmitter becomes unresponsive until the battery is disconnected — even when the operator isn't trying to bind. V1 receivers don't trigger this because their first acks aren't in the same format. The fix is structural: while unbound the V2 keeps autoACK off and the ack-payload FIFO empty, so it looks exactly like 'no receiver present' to a scanning TX. The instant a real bind packet arrives, tryBind() flips both flags back on and the standard bind handshake completes — confirmed working in the field.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.65-bind-wait
versions.notes: Fix for the bind page hanging after 'Clear bind & restart'. The form used a native HTML POST and relied on the chip's reply landing before reboot — on a slow phone link the reply was cut off and the page sat there forever. Submit is now driven from JavaScript: the page swaps to a local 'Rebooting…' panel with its own poll loop, fires the POST as fire-and-forget, then polls /api/state.json until the receiver answers and redirects home. No more stuck spinner; transmitter binding itself was always working.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.64-safe-area
versions.notes: iPhone PWA fix — the model name at the top of every page now sits clear of the Dynamic Island / notch when the chip's web UI is added to the Home Screen and opened fullscreen. body padding switches to max(1.5em, env(safe-area-inset-top)) so regular browsers and desktops keep their existing spacing while notched iPhones get whatever inset iOS asks for.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.63-show-password
versions.notes: Show-password checkbox on the first-run gate, and autocomplete hints (username for SSID, current-password for password) on both forms so iOS Safari is more likely to offer its Wi-Fi-password autofill flow.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.62-factory-reset
versions.notes: New 'Factory defaults' card on /wifi. Clears every NVS key (WiFi credentials, model name, bind state, output protocol, board MAC, dev manifest URL) plus the Rotorflight backup files, then reboots. Use before passing the receiver to a new owner.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.61-firstrun-combo
versions.notes: Streamlined first-time setup. The welcome card now accepts model name and (optional) home-WiFi credentials in a single form — one POST, one reboot, and the chip comes up on the home network under the new name straight away. Saves a reboot and saves the phone from having to rejoin a renamed AP.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.60-name-rules
versions.notes: Tightens model-name validation after a user got stranded by a name with a space. Form now uses an HTML5 pattern + live JS check + chip-side rejection — letters, digits and hyphens only, starts with alphanumeric, no spaces. The welcome card spells the rules out, and a live preview shows exactly what hostname / AP SSID the chosen name will become.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.59-model-name
versions.notes: Each receiver can now carry its own model name (e.g. 'Goblin 700'). On fresh boot the chip auto-names itself 'RXV2-XXXX' using the last 4 hex of its board MAC, so multiple receivers no longer collide on the same SSID/hostname. Tap the title on the home page (or the amber warning banner when the default name is in use) to set a friendly name. Saving reboots and re-derives the soft-AP SSID, mDNS hostname, and page titles from the new name. Phones in the area see 'Goblin 700' and 'T-Rex 500' in their WiFi list instead of two 'LDRC_RX's, and mDNS now resolves 'goblin-700.local' independently per board.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.58-swap-50ms
versions.notes: Radio-swap trigger and cooldown shortened from 200 ms to 50 ms each, matching V1's more responsive behaviour. At the V1 protocol's ~125 Hz packet rate that's about 6 missed packets before the chip tries the next transceiver — quick enough to catch a real fade or antenna shadow without thrashing on routine 1–2-packet gaps. Reported by user: V1 sometimes saw several swaps while the longest gap stayed below 100 ms; the new threshold restores that responsiveness.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.57-help-text
versions.notes: Every page now has a small floating ? help button in the top-right corner. Tap it to read a short, friendly explanation of what the page does, how to use it, and any tips. Covers all 15 pages — Home, Fly, View channels, Black box, Bind, Firmware, WiFi, Output protocol, Rotorflight, and the five Rotorflight sub-pages (PID, PID+, Rates, Governor profile, Governor global, Backups). Tap outside the panel or the 'Got it' button to dismiss. New users no longer need to guess what a page does.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.56-csn-park
versions.notes: Fixes triple-transceiver detection on the new prototype board. runRadioSelfTest() only parked slot 1's CSN HIGH at boot — slots 2 and 3's CSN pins were left in INPUT mode (default), and the nRF24L01+ has no internal CSN pull-up, so their pins could drift LOW through PCB leakage. The result: when slot 1 first drove SPI, multiple chips were selected at once, MISO got clobbered, slot 1's self-test failed, and the chip declared radio 1 missing even though its hardware was perfectly fine. Symptom on the prototype: one radio installed = bind worked; two or three installed = slot 1 'not present'. Now all six radio control pins (three CSN + three CE) are explicitly OUTPUT-deselected before any SPI traffic begins.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.55-strut-bend
versions.notes: Helicopter skid struts now bend inward at the top so they actually touch the body, the way real model heli landing gear does. Built with TubeGeometry along a Catmull-Rom curve — foot at the skid, short vertical run, then a smooth inward bend that lands the strut top on the body's lower side. The straight cylinders left the struts floating beside the cabin.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.54-no-rejoin
versions.notes: Removes the 'Rejoin WiFi' button added yesterday — with AP+STA running in parallel and STA retrying forever in the background it was a placebo button. The chip already does what the button asked for, automatically and continuously.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.53-banner-fix
versions.notes: Home-page firmware banner now uses the chip's /api/firmware/check (same data source as /firmware) so the two screens always agree. The old banner code only looked at the local dev server and could disagree with /firmware when only the public mirror had something new — confusing 'update available' messages that vanished on click. Removes the hardcoded m4macmini.local manifest URL too, so end users without a dev server get clean behaviour instead of a silent fetch failure.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.52-ap-always-rejoin
versions.notes: AP and STA now run simultaneously from boot. The soft-AP `LDRC_RX` is always live at 192.168.4.1, web server + mDNS + MSP bridge come up immediately, and STA tries the home network in the background — when STA succeeds the chip is reachable at both 192.168.4.1 and 192.168.1.231. Overnight router hiccups can no longer strand the chip in AP-only mode because there's no auto-fallback path any more: timeouts just trigger another STA attempt. Also: new 'Rejoin WiFi' button on the /wifi page that retries the home network using the saved password — no need to re-type credentials when the chip is in AP after a long drop. The 3-second STA-drop debounce filters out the spurious WL_DISCONNECTED reports that the ESP32 throws during background scans.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.50-sta-forever
versions.notes: WiFi behaviour rewritten so the chip stops surrendering to AP mode on transient stalls. NVS credentials are never being touched (the boot log confirms this on every reboot) — what was happening is the home router occasionally took longer than the chip's STA-join budget to recognise the freshly-rebooted chip, and the chip then auto-fell to AP. New policy: on a timeout the chip resets the radio (mode OFF, settle, mode STA) and tries again FOREVER. AP fallback only fires on a genuine `WL_CONNECT_FAILED` (wrong password) or persistent `WL_NO_SSID_AVAIL` after three tries (network truly missing). Also turns off WiFi.persistent (no cached BSSID across reboots) and sets max TX power for better association on weak corners of the house. Quick-boot recovery (three power cycles in a row) is still the escape hatch if the chip ever needs to be forced into AP for reconfiguration.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.49-heli-curves
versions.notes: Heli preview tightened up per feedback. Skids now follow a curved path (TubeGeometry along a Catmull-Rom curve) so the front sweeps up off the ground like real model landing gear. The side stripe is a swept chevron — bulges through the middle and tapers to a sharp point at the nose — rendered with ShapeGeometry rather than the flat rectangle. The rectangular black chassis block between the skids and the diagonal boom support strut have both been removed; the latter was reading as a stray aerial poking out the back of the body.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.48-heli-cleaner
versions.notes: Helicopter preview cleaned up per feedback: the bright red battery block under the chassis is gone, the yellow band around the body is now a painted side-decal (flat PlaneGeometry on each flank) rather than a 3D torus ring sticking out, the horizontal stabiliser is removed entirely, and the vertical fin now hangs down beneath the tail boom instead of up above it. JS syntax checked with `node --check` before flashing.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.47-heli-fix
versions.notes: Fixes the 'Loading 3D model…' hang on the diagnostics page caused by a name collision in the v0.9.46 helicopter redesign — the new tail-boom support strut shared a variable name (`strutGeo`) with the existing skid struts, so the script threw `SyntaxError: Identifier already declared` and the heli never rendered. Renamed to `boomStrutGeo` and added a node --check pass to the workflow so this kind of regression is caught before flashing.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.46-sab-style
versions.notes: Heli preview redesigned to read as a real RC model rather than a generic streamlined pod. New: visible carbon chassis between body and skids with a bright red LiPo battery slung underneath, a sharp pointed nose cone projecting forward of the canopy, a tail-boom support strut running diagonally back to the chassis, a chunky tail gearbox housing at the boom tip, a swashplate disc under a beefier rotor head with two cylindrical blade grips at right angles. Colour scheme switched to SAB Heli Division-style: glossy black body, vivid lime-yellow stripe and tail surfaces, glossy black canopy. Battery is bright red for contrast against the chassis.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.45-fresh-pages
versions.notes: HTML pages now ship with Cache-Control: no-cache so a stale page from the previous firmware can't keep showing after an update. Phones still send a normal request each visit; the chip re-streams the (tiny) HTML and you always see what's actually on disk. Static assets (style.css, app.js, three.min.js, the backdrop jpg / svg) keep their aggressive immutable caching — they're versioned via ?v= cache busters anyway.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.44-sta-patient
versions.notes: Stops the chip from falling to AP mode on a transient WiFi hiccup. STA join now retries 5 times at 25 seconds per attempt — ~2 minutes total before AP fallback — which is plenty to outlast any router-side stall (stale DHCP lease, channel congestion, etc.). The attempt counter is reset on every successful join so a mid-session disconnect later doesn't inherit the boot-time count. Boot now logs the actual NVS state (SSID name, password presence, bind state) to the in-RAM event log too, so you can verify on the Black-box page whether credentials really are saved without needing a USB serial cable.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.43-heli-slim
versions.notes: Tail-rotor disc now sits in the same plane as the blades it replaces (the v0.9.42 disc was accidentally tipped 90° out and read edge-on). Fuselage cross-section more than halved in width (0.95 → 0.48 Z-scale) and lengthened so the body reads as a sleek model pod rather than a fat torpedo. Slimmer accent stripe, narrower canopy with a longer pointed nose, lower-profile cowling, and a slender push-rod running along the boom to the tail-rotor pitch slider.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.42-heli-real
versions.notes: Helicopter 3-D preview on the diagnostics page made to look like a real model. Fuselage slimmed and lengthened, canopy pushed forward and narrowed, engine cowling rounded, tail boom slimmed and stretched so the tail rotor sits well aft. Main and tail rotor blades remain solid bars only at spool-up speeds — once they cross the persistence-of-vision threshold (~3 RPS) the animation hides the discrete blades and renders a translucent disc with a slightly darker tip ring, matching how the eye actually perceives a spinning rotor. Default RPM scaled to realistic flight values.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.41-reboot-redirect
versions.notes: Post-reboot pages no longer leave you staring at a dead 'Rebooting…' screen. Save-and-reboot handlers (WiFi credentials, output protocol, bind clear, OTA rollback, manual reboot) now render a live status line that polls /api/state.json once a second after a 4-second grace period; the moment the chip answers, the page redirects to /. If the chip never returns within 60 s — typically meaning a wrong WiFi password sent it to AP mode — a clear recovery message appears with the AP SSID and IP. Fly-mode opts out of the auto-reload since it deliberately cuts WiFi.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.40-wifi-safer
versions.notes: WiFi robustness + safety tweaks. The dangerous 'Forget & reboot' button is gone — the same outcome (back to AP) is reachable by entering a different SSID/password through the normal save form, so the explicit wipe button was needless rope. STA join attempts now retry once before falling back to AP and the per-attempt timeout is bumped from 12 s to 18 s, which kills the 'WiFi password lost' false alarms where the credentials in NVS were intact but a slow router-side reconnect tripped the AP fallback. Boot now also logs what is actually in NVS (SSID, password presence, bind pipe, proto), so the next time something does look wiped we'll know within one reboot whether NVS really was disturbed or just the join timed out.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.39-mown-turf
versions.notes: New backdrop photo — Scott Webb, “Landscape of grass field under blue sky” (White Oaks Park, London, Canada; Unsplash License). Short manicured turf reads as a flying field rather than the wild meadow of the previous backdrop. Resized 6000×4000 → 1800×1200, JPEG 70% (~470 KB).

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.38-real-photo
versions.notes: Real photograph for the page backdrop — a free-licence Unsplash image (Wolfgang Hasselmann, “Blue sky with white clouds over a green field”), resized to 1800×1200 and re-encoded at JPEG 78% (~340 KB). The hand-drawn SVG is kept on the chip as a fallback. Ships with dev/uploadfs_safe.sh and dev/preserve_backups.py so future filesystem updates don’t wipe the receiver’s saved Rotorflight backups.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.37-flying-field
versions.notes: Photo-realistic flying-field backdrop rolled out across every receiver page. Sky uses a seven-stop gradient with a warm haze band; cumulus clouds carved by Perlin-noise filters with diffuse-lit highlights and shadowed undersides; the meadow has a noise-textured base, a tileable blade pattern, and ~70 hand-drawn foreground blades.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.35-page-cards
versions.notes: Update page redesigned in the ReedsV2 style. Currently-installed card with status pill; per-version cards showing the clean version number, release codename pill, and Newer/Older/Current tag; install/reinstall/roll-back button on each card. The local dev server is auto-discovered at m4macmini.local:8000.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.34-fw-tidy
versions.notes: Slimmer /firmware page: just the version numbers as buttons (green = update, amber = rollback). Local-server section hidden unless reachable.

versions.name: RXV2-0.9.33-ota-public
versions.notes: First release reachable from messiter.com — adds an over-the-air update path that no longer requires the dev Mac to be on the LAN.
